passage.text:
IELTS Reading Practice
Business & Economics
10 Reading Passages  ·  500–600 words each  ·  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)

Contents
Passage 1: How Businesses Work
Passage 2: Globalisation — A Connected World Economy
Passage 3: Marketing — How Companies Sell Their Products
Passage 4: Banks and How They Work
Passage 5: Entrepreneurship — Starting Your Own Business
Passage 6: Supply and Demand — The Basics of Economics
Passage 7: The Workplace — How Work Is Changing
Passage 8: Advertising — How It Influences What We Buy
Passage 9: Poverty and Inequality — The Global Economic Divide
Passage 10: Consumer Rights — Protecting Shoppers

Passage 1: How Businesses Work
Topic: Business Basics  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
A business is an organisation that produces or sells goods or services in order to make money. Businesses come in many different sizes and types. A small local bakery, a family-run restaurant, and a large international company like Apple or Toyota are all businesses. What they have in common is that they all try to earn more money than they spend — this is called making a "profit." If a business spends more than it earns, it makes a "loss." Businesses that make losses for a long time will eventually close down. Running a successful business requires careful planning, good management, and an understanding of what customers want and need.
Paragraph B
There are several types of business ownership. A sole trader is a business owned and run by one person — for example, a freelance photographer or a self-employed plumber. This is the simplest type of business to set up, but the owner takes all the risks personally. A partnership is when two or more people own and run a business together — for example, a law firm or a medical practice. Partners share the profits but also share the risks. A limited company is a business that is legally separate from its owners. The owners — called shareholders — invest money in the business and receive a share of the profits, but their personal money is protected if the business fails.
Paragraph C
Every business needs customers — people who buy its products or services. To attract customers, businesses use marketing. Marketing includes everything a business does to promote itself and its products, from advertising on television and social media, to offering discounts, to designing eye-catching product packaging. One important part of marketing is understanding who your customers are — their age, interests, income, and habits. This information helps businesses decide what products to make, what price to charge, and how and where to advertise. In today's world, digital marketing — using websites, email, and social media — is increasingly important because so many people spend time online.
Paragraph D
Starting a new business — called a "start-up" — is exciting but also very risky. Many new businesses fail within the first few years. The most common reasons for failure include not having enough money to keep the business going, not understanding what customers want, having too much competition, or simply poor management. To improve their chances of success, people starting a new business are often advised to write a detailed business plan. A business plan sets out what the business will sell, who its customers will be, how it will make money, and how much money it will need to get started. Banks and investors use business plans to decide whether to lend money to new businesses.

Passage 2: Globalisation — A Connected World Economy
Topic: Globalisation & Trade  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Globalisation is the process by which countries, businesses, and people around the world become more closely connected. Over the past few decades, improvements in transport, technology, and communication have made it much easier and cheaper to move goods, money, and information across borders. As a result, international trade — buying and selling products between countries — has grown enormously. A smartphone made in China uses parts from South Korea, software from the United States, and minerals from Africa. The clothes we wear, the food we eat, and the technology we use every day often come from many different countries. This level of global connection was almost impossible to imagine just a hundred years ago.
Paragraph B
Globalisation has brought many benefits. For businesses, it has opened up huge new markets. A small company can now sell its products to customers all over the world through the internet. For consumers, globalisation has made a much wider range of goods available, often at lower prices, because companies can produce in countries where costs are lower. For developing countries, it has created jobs and helped millions of people escape poverty. Countries like China, South Korea, Vietnam, and Bangladesh have experienced rapid economic growth partly because of their involvement in global trade and manufacturing. The sharing of ideas, technology, and knowledge across borders has also helped countries develop faster.
Paragraph C
However, globalisation also has negative effects. One major concern is the loss of jobs in richer countries. When companies move their factories to countries where wages are lower — a practice called "offshoring" — workers in the home country can lose their jobs. For example, many manufacturing jobs in the United States and the United Kingdom have moved to Asia over the past few decades. Another concern is the effect on the environment. Moving goods around the world by ship and plane produces a lot of greenhouse gas emissions. Also, when companies move to countries with weaker environmental laws, they may pollute more than they would be allowed to at home. Globalisation can also threaten local cultures, as people in different countries increasingly watch the same films, wear the same brands, and eat the same fast food.
Paragraph D
In recent years, some countries have begun to question whether globalisation has gone too far. The COVID-19 pandemic showed what can happen when global supply chains — the networks of companies that produce and deliver goods — are disrupted. When factories in one country closed, it caused shortages of essential goods — including medicines and medical equipment — in other countries around the world. This led some governments to consider producing more things at home rather than depending on other countries. Similarly, political tensions between large economies such as the United States and China have led to new barriers to trade. Despite these challenges, most economists still believe that international trade and cooperation are essential for global prosperity and development.

Passage 3: Marketing — How Companies Sell Their Products
Topic: Marketing & Advertising  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Marketing is everything a company does to find customers and persuade them to buy its products or services. Without marketing, even the best products might never be found by the people who need them. In the past, marketing mainly meant advertising in newspapers, on television, or on billboards. Today, marketing has changed enormously because of the internet and social media. Companies now use websites, email newsletters, social media accounts, and online advertising to reach customers directly. Digital marketing has made it possible for even very small businesses to reach a large audience at a relatively low cost, and has given companies more precise ways to target specific groups of customers.
Paragraph B
One of the most important tools in marketing is understanding your target market — the specific group of people most likely to buy your product. Different products appeal to different groups of people. A company selling baby products will target new parents. A company selling sports equipment will target people who are active and interested in fitness. Once a company understands its target market, it can decide how to reach those people most effectively. For example, a company targeting teenagers will probably focus its marketing on social media platforms like TikTok or Instagram, while a company targeting older adults might prefer television or newspaper advertisements.
Paragraph C
Branding is another very important part of marketing. A brand is more than just a logo or a name — it is the overall image and feeling that a company creates in the minds of its customers. Strong brands make customers feel something when they think of a company — trust, quality, excitement, or luxury. Apple, for example, has built a brand associated with creativity and high quality. Nike has built a brand associated with sporting achievement and motivation. Companies invest enormous amounts of money in building and protecting their brand, because a strong brand makes customers more likely to choose their products over competitors and to remain loyal customers over time.
Paragraph D
In recent years, social media influencers have become an increasingly important marketing tool. An influencer is a person who has a large number of followers on social media and who can persuade their followers to buy products by recommending them. Companies pay influencers — who might be celebrities, fitness experts, travel bloggers, or even ordinary people with large online followings — to promote their products in posts and videos. This type of marketing is effective because followers often trust the influencer's opinions more than they trust traditional advertising. However, there are concerns about transparency — followers do not always know when a post is a paid advertisement rather than a genuine personal recommendation. Many countries now have rules requiring influencers to clearly label paid promotions.

Passage 4: Banks and How They Work
Topic: Finance & Banking  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Banks are one of the most important institutions in any modern economy. A bank is a business that accepts money from customers — called "deposits" — keeps it safe, and lends it to other customers who need to borrow money. Banks make most of their money from the difference between the interest rate they charge people who borrow money and the interest rate they pay to people who save money with them. For example, a bank might pay savers 2% interest on their savings each year, while charging borrowers 5% interest on loans. The 3% difference is the bank's profit. Without banks, it would be very difficult for businesses to grow, for people to buy houses, or for governments to fund public services.
Paragraph B
Banks offer many different services to their customers. The most basic is a current account — a place to keep money that can be accessed easily for everyday spending. Banks also offer savings accounts, where money earns interest over time. One of the most important services banks provide is loans — lending money to individuals and businesses. Personal loans help people pay for things like cars or home improvements. Mortgages are long-term loans that help people buy a house, usually paid back over 25 to 30 years. Business loans help companies invest in new equipment, hire staff, or expand into new markets. Without access to loans, many people and businesses would be unable to make the investments they need to grow and prosper.
Paragraph C
Central banks play a special and very important role in the economy. Most countries have a central bank — for example, the Bank of England in the United Kingdom, the Federal Reserve in the United States, or the European Central Bank in the Eurozone. Unlike ordinary banks, a central bank does not usually deal directly with ordinary customers. Instead, its main job is to control the amount of money in the economy and to set the key interest rates that all other banks follow. When the economy is growing too fast and prices are rising too quickly — a situation called inflation — the central bank usually raises interest rates to slow down borrowing and spending. When the economy is weak, it may lower interest rates to encourage people and businesses to borrow and spend more.
Paragraph D
In recent years, technology has transformed banking. Most people now manage their money through online banking apps on their smartphones, without ever needing to visit a physical bank branch. This has made banking much more convenient, but it has also led to the closure of many bank branches — particularly in smaller towns and rural areas — which has caused problems for older people or those without access to the internet. A newer development is the rise of "fintech" companies — financial technology businesses that offer banking-style services without being traditional banks. Apps like Revolut and N26 allow people to open accounts, make payments, and exchange currencies entirely on their phones. These services are often cheaper and faster than those offered by traditional banks.

Passage 5: Entrepreneurship — Starting Your Own Business
Topic: Entrepreneurship  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
An entrepreneur is a person who starts a new business, taking on financial risks in the hope of making a profit. Entrepreneurs play a very important role in the economy. They create new businesses, develop new products and services, create jobs, and drive economic growth. Famous examples of entrepreneurs include Elon Musk, who founded SpaceX and Tesla, and Mark Zuckerberg, who created Facebook. However, entrepreneurship is not just for famous billionaires. Every year, millions of ordinary people around the world start small businesses — opening a shop, starting a catering company, or launching an online service. All of them are entrepreneurs.
Paragraph B
Starting a new business requires several important qualities. Most entrepreneurs are creative — they come up with new ideas or find better ways to solve existing problems. They are also resilient — able to deal with setbacks and failures without giving up. Starting a business rarely goes smoothly at first, and the ability to learn from mistakes and keep going is essential. Entrepreneurs also need to be good at managing risk. Starting a business always involves uncertainty — there is no guarantee of success. A good entrepreneur thinks carefully about what could go wrong and makes plans to manage these risks. Strong communication skills are also important, as entrepreneurs need to persuade customers, investors, and employees to believe in their idea.
Paragraph C
One of the biggest challenges for new entrepreneurs is finding enough money to get started. Starting a business usually requires an initial investment — money spent on equipment, premises, stock, staff, or marketing before the business starts earning money. There are several sources of funding available. Personal savings are the most common starting point. Friends and family often provide early support. Banks can provide business loans, though they usually require a detailed business plan and some form of security. "Angel investors" are wealthy individuals who invest in early-stage businesses in exchange for a share of the company. "Venture capital" firms invest larger amounts in businesses that have the potential to grow very quickly. In recent years, "crowdfunding" platforms — websites where large numbers of people each contribute a small amount of money — have become a popular way to raise funds for new ideas.
Paragraph D
Governments in many countries actively support entrepreneurship because it creates jobs and drives economic growth. Many governments offer tax benefits, grants, and low-interest loans to help people start businesses. Business support organisations and "incubators" — programmes that provide workspace, advice, and training for early-stage businesses — also play an important role. In some countries, entrepreneurship education is now being introduced into schools, so that young people develop the skills and attitudes needed to start their own businesses in the future. Despite all the support available, entrepreneurship remains a challenging path. Research consistently shows that around half of all new businesses fail within their first five years. But for those who succeed, starting a business can bring great personal and financial rewards.

Passage 6: Supply and Demand — The Basics of Economics
Topic: Economics  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Supply and demand is one of the most fundamental ideas in economics. "Demand" refers to how much of a product or service people want to buy at a given price. "Supply" refers to how much of a product producers are willing and able to make and sell at a given price. The relationship between supply and demand determines the price of almost everything we buy — from food and clothes to houses and cars. When many people want something and there is not enough of it available, the price tends to go up. When there is more of something than people want to buy, the price tends to come down. This process helps "balance" the market over time.
Paragraph B
Demand for a product is affected by several factors. The most obvious is price — in general, when a product becomes more expensive, fewer people buy it, and when it becomes cheaper, more people buy it. But price is not the only factor. The income of consumers matters — when people earn more money, they tend to buy more things. Advertising can increase demand by making people aware of a product or making them want it more. The price of related products also matters. If the price of coffee goes up, some people may switch to tea — this is called a "substitute product." And if the price of printers goes down, demand for printer ink is likely to increase — this is called a "complementary product."
Paragraph C
Supply is also affected by various factors. The cost of production is one of the most important — if the cost of making something goes up, for example because wages or raw material prices increase, producers may make less of it or raise prices. New technology can increase supply by making production cheaper and more efficient. Government policies such as taxes and subsidies also affect supply. A tax on a product makes it more expensive to produce and can reduce supply. A subsidy — money given by the government to help produce something — makes production cheaper and can increase supply. Natural events such as bad weather can also affect the supply of food — a drought or flood can destroy crops and reduce the amount of food available.
Paragraph D
In the real world, prices are not always set purely by supply and demand. Governments sometimes control prices to protect consumers or producers. For example, a government might set a minimum wage — the lowest amount an employer is allowed to pay a worker — to make sure workers are paid fairly. It might also set price controls on essential items like fuel or food to prevent prices from rising so high that poor people cannot afford them. On the other hand, businesses that are very large and have few competitors — called "monopolies" — can sometimes charge high prices because customers have no choice but to pay. To prevent this, governments in many countries have special laws to control the behaviour of monopolies and protect fair competition.

Passage 7: The Workplace — How Work Is Changing
Topic: Work & Employment  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
The world of work is changing faster than at any time in recent history. For most of the twentieth century, a typical working life followed a fairly predictable pattern — you studied, then found a job with a company, worked for the same employer for many years, and eventually retired with a pension. Today, this pattern is much less common. People now change jobs more frequently, often working for several different employers over their career. Many people also change careers entirely, moving from one industry to a completely different one. The rise of technology, globalisation, and new ways of working have all contributed to these changes, and many experts predict that the workplace of the future will look very different from the one we know today.
Paragraph B
One of the biggest recent changes in working life is the growth of remote work — working from home or another location outside the office. During the COVID-19 pandemic, millions of people who had previously always worked in an office suddenly had to work from home. After the pandemic, many employees chose to continue working remotely, at least some of the time, and many employers have now adopted flexible working arrangements. Remote work has many advantages — employees save time and money on commuting, have more flexibility in managing their time, and often report higher job satisfaction. However, it also has disadvantages — some people find it harder to separate work from personal life, feel more isolated, or miss the social aspects of being in an office.
Paragraph C
Another major trend is the rise of the "gig economy" — a way of working where people do short-term or freelance jobs rather than having a permanent full-time job with one employer. Platforms like Uber, Deliveroo, and Fiverr connect workers directly with customers or clients, allowing people to work whenever they want and take on as many or as few jobs as they like. The gig economy offers workers great flexibility and independence. However, gig workers often do not have the same rights and protections as regular employees — they may not receive paid holiday, sick pay, or pension contributions. Critics argue that the gig economy benefits companies at the expense of workers, while supporters say it gives people freedom and choice.
Paragraph D
Technology — particularly artificial intelligence and automation — is also changing which jobs exist and what skills employers look for. Some jobs that were previously done by people — such as data entry, simple manufacturing tasks, and basic customer service — are now increasingly done by machines and software. This has led to concern about unemployment and the future of work. However, technology also creates new types of jobs. Web designers, data analysts, social media managers, and app developers are all jobs that barely existed twenty years ago. Most experts believe that the jobs of the future will require strong creative, social, and problem-solving skills — the kinds of skills that are currently difficult for machines to replicate. This means that education and lifelong learning will be more important than ever.

Passage 8: Advertising — How It Influences What We Buy
Topic: Advertising & Consumer Behaviour  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Advertising is everywhere. We see it on television, on our smartphones, on the sides of buses, in magazines, and on the streets. The purpose of advertising is to make people aware of a product or service and to persuade them to buy it. Companies around the world spend enormous amounts of money on advertising — global advertising spending is over 700 billion US dollars per year. This shows how important companies believe advertising is to their success. But how exactly does advertising work? And how does it influence the choices we make when we go shopping or browse online?
Paragraph B
Advertisers use many different techniques to influence consumers. One of the most powerful is emotional appeal — making the audience feel a certain way when they see an advertisement. Advertisements for cars often show images of freedom and adventure. Perfume advertisements often suggest romance and luxury. Baby product advertisements show warmth and care. By connecting their product to positive emotions and feelings, companies hope that people will associate those good feelings with their brand. Another common technique is using celebrities or well-known people to recommend products. When a famous athlete wears a certain sports brand, or a popular actor recommends a skincare product, many consumers are more likely to trust and buy that product.
Paragraph C
In recent years, digital advertising has become the most important form of advertising for many companies. Unlike traditional advertising on television or in newspapers — which shows the same advertisement to a very large, mixed audience — digital advertising allows companies to target very specific groups of people. Websites and social media platforms collect enormous amounts of data about their users — what they search for, what they click on, what they buy, and even how long they spend looking at each page. This data is used to show each person advertisements that match their specific interests and behaviour. This targeted advertising is much more effective than traditional advertising, because the audience is already likely to be interested in the product.
Paragraph D
There are growing concerns about the power of advertising, particularly its effects on children and young people. Young people are heavy users of social media and are exposed to advertising constantly. Research has found that children are often unable to recognise when content they see online is actually a paid advertisement rather than genuine content from someone they follow. There are also concerns about advertising that promotes unhealthy products — such as junk food and sugary drinks — to young audiences. Many countries have introduced rules to limit certain types of advertising directed at children. There are also increasing concerns about privacy, as digital advertising relies on collecting large amounts of personal data about people's behaviour, often without them fully understanding how their information is being used.

Passage 9: Poverty and Inequality — The Global Economic Divide
Topic: Economics & Society  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Poverty is one of the most serious problems in the world today. A person is considered to be living in "extreme poverty" if they survive on less than 2.15 US dollars per day — the international poverty line set by the World Bank. While the number of people living in extreme poverty has fallen significantly over the past few decades — from around 36% of the world's population in 1990 to about 9% today — it still affects hundreds of millions of people, mainly in parts of sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. Poverty means more than just having little money. People in poverty often lack access to education, healthcare, clean water, safe housing, and adequate food — all of which makes it very difficult to improve their situation.
Paragraph B
Inequality — the gap between the richest and poorest people in society — is also a significant problem. Even in wealthy countries, inequality has been growing in recent decades. The richest 1% of the world's population now owns more wealth than the bottom 50% combined. This extreme concentration of wealth raises important questions about fairness and justice. High levels of inequality can damage society in many ways. Research has shown that countries with greater inequality tend to have higher rates of crime, worse health outcomes, lower levels of social trust, and weaker economic growth over time. Inequality also makes it harder for people from poor backgrounds to move up in society — a concept known as "social mobility."
Paragraph C
There are many causes of poverty and inequality. In developing countries, poverty is often caused by a lack of economic development, limited access to education, weak institutions, and in some cases conflict and political instability. In wealthier countries, inequality has been driven by factors such as technological change — which has increased demand for highly skilled workers while reducing demand for low-skilled ones — and globalisation, which has moved many manufacturing jobs to lower-wage countries. Tax systems can also contribute to inequality if they are designed in ways that benefit wealthy people and large corporations more than ordinary workers. Discrimination based on gender, race, or background can also prevent some people from having equal opportunities to succeed.
Paragraph D
Many approaches have been used to try to reduce poverty and inequality. International aid — money given by wealthy countries to help poorer ones — has funded schools, hospitals, and infrastructure in developing countries. Conditional cash transfer programmes — which give money directly to poor families on the condition that their children attend school and receive regular health check-ups — have been shown to be effective in several countries. In wealthy countries, progressive taxation — where richer people pay a higher percentage of their income in tax — combined with strong public services such as free healthcare and education, can help reduce inequality. Investing in education and skills training gives people from poorer backgrounds a better chance of improving their economic situation. Many experts agree that reducing inequality and poverty requires both economic growth and deliberate policies to ensure the benefits of growth are shared fairly.

Passage 10: Consumer Rights — Protecting Shoppers
Topic: Consumer Affairs  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Every time we buy something — whether it is a loaf of bread, a new phone, or a holiday — we are acting as a consumer. Consumers have rights — legal protections that help make sure they are treated fairly by the companies they buy from. Consumer rights cover many situations: what happens if a product you buy is faulty or does not work as described, what information a company must give you before you buy, and what you can do if a service you paid for is not delivered properly. Without consumer protection laws, businesses could easily mislead buyers, sell unsafe products, or refuse to give money back when things go wrong.
Paragraph B
Most countries have laws that give consumers basic rights when they buy goods. In many countries, if a product you buy is faulty — meaning it does not work properly or is broken — you have the right to get a repair, a replacement, or a refund. The product must also be "as described" — it must match what the seller said it was, whether in an advertisement, a label, or a verbal description. Products must also be safe to use. Companies that sell unsafe products can be fined heavily or forced to recall the product — that is, take it back from customers and stop selling it. Online shopping has made these rights even more important, as consumers often cannot inspect a product before buying it.
Paragraph C
As well as rights related to the products themselves, consumers also have rights related to the information they receive before making a purchase. Companies are generally required to give clear, honest, and accurate information about their products and services — including the price, any additional fees, and the key terms and conditions. Misleading advertising — for example, claiming a product can do something it cannot — is illegal in most countries. When buying online, consumers in many countries have the right to change their mind and return a product within a set period, even if there is nothing wrong with it. This is sometimes called a "cooling-off period," and it is particularly important for purchases made online, where consumers may not have seen or tried the product before buying.
Paragraph D
If a consumer feels that their rights have not been respected, there are several steps they can take. The first step is usually to contact the company directly — most companies have a customer service department that handles complaints. If the company does not resolve the problem satisfactorily, consumers can contact an independent organisation that helps resolve disputes — these are sometimes called "ombudsmen" or consumer protection agencies. In many countries, consumers can also take companies to a small claims court to recover money for faulty goods or services. Consumer organisations — which are independent groups that test products, investigate companies, and represent consumers' interests — also play an important role in protecting shoppers. Being an informed consumer — knowing your rights and what to do when things go wrong — is one of the most important forms of self-protection in today's market.
